title: What a long strange journey it has been
display_title: What a long strange journey it has been
wikitext:
[]: What a long strange journey it has been...             Quote Post by Augustus » Wed Jul 17, 2013 8:56 am Hello everyone! I apologize for the long post and for just blitzing into these message boards for a single personal entry without contributing otherwise to this splendid community, but I honestly felt like I had to share my feelings. It was 1997, a day like any other for me, I was 17 and I was living my life as a model and rather competitive student, good and hyped only child, reasonably mature in intelligence but very green and behind in personal experiences. In Italy a few bold publishers were translating manga, and I was reading a good deal of what was coming out, together with a good deal of Italian native novels. I also had been watching anime for years, I would never hide that they have been the closest companions of my childhood. The first volume of Evangelion came out and without knowing what it was, I bought it and I kept buying the (then monthly) issues for about a year. I liked the genre, but I had a sense of discomfort with the introspection and the complexities as I was all about entertainment and everything beyond fights among cyborgs was a sort of filler I unhappily accepted as a "typical and recurring Japanese problem with their kids". The anime (original series) was available for home-video, but never watched it until it came on TV. It was 2001, I was at university, less green and with a rejection to everything which was not real life due to the burdensome commitment with the studies and the living away from home, which I was trying to offset with sports that allowed me to exhaust tension and aggression. I spent little time with the TV and I was lousily and boredly following the episodes, missing some here and there without caring much. Where I was looking for a final epic battle, I got an emotional internal struggle (episodes 25-26 aired in 2002 but I watched them later on since I was studying in the US at that time) and thought that I did not miss much when I quit purchasing the manga, that I later learned was progressing at an erratic and anyway dead slow pace. Time passed by, 11 years of work experiences, job satisfaction, job nightmares, travels, joy, family dramas, friends from old becoming fathers or mothers, perfect loves that lasted a heartbeat, painful loves that lasted too long, things that I regret doing, or not doing, growing up (whatever it means), in two words, medals and scars, as for everyone on this little planet. In all this, I got to know a very nice Japanese girl in India last year, with whom I shared a few drinks under the Monsoon, some business chit-chat and some memories as teenagers. Surprisingly, we ended up talking about how cathartic was to watch giant robots fighting aliens, and then she mentioned Evangelion anime, which she had recently re-watched after her first child, because somehow she deeply associated the series with life and wanted to experience it with the eyes of her in the early 30s. She talked about the various endings (of which I was unaware) and I cut it short saying that I was no longer into anime, but would have liked to read the manga. She mentioned that the story there was different too and that it was still ongoing, to which I replied that it was bad luck then, because I was not much into waiting who knows how long for the ending to be out. She politely told me that we would have let me know when it would have been the case. Last week she did it. Considering it to be one of those catchy situations where you sort of have to follow the tide, I browsed the net a bit and in a few days I got ahold of the whole original 26 episodes, the two 1997 movies, all English-translated paper manga and the last chapters translated by the fans, and devoted the whole week-end to a full immersion. It has been a powerful experience. The fights I was so focusing on years ago became just part of the plot, and the insights and complexities I was ignoring became the pieces of the message, scrambled as a puzzle until the very ending, where I felt a warm sense of hope and strength. To me, the whole story is about whether our lives and our world albeit imperfect are worth living and fighting for. Every ending, as different they may be, brings the same answer, which is yes. All antagonists bidding on a different answer, at the end, lose. Shinji and Asuka, close to be overwhelmed by guilt, loneliness, lack of confidence, delusions, responsibilities which would crush any adult, refuse apathy and oblivion to live for a chance of joy and fulfillment and ready to pay the cost in terms of pain and suffering. A few flashes below. The path that leads to such decision varies between anime and manga and within the anime between releases, but different ways lead to the same conclusion. The different outcome of the fight between Eva 02 and the MP Evas in The End of Evangelion and in the manga (with or without the intervention of Shinji), provides different nuances to the relationship between Asuka and Shinji and probably represents different "candies" to the fans, but in the overall scheme make no difference, because the two were close enough even in the more crude and less sympathetic scenario. The precise interpretation of the endings in which the whole stage congratulates with Shinji, or Asuka caresses him or shake his hand during an iconic Winter in Tokyo, makes little difference, because they are there. They decided to live life and fight for their future. Do you prefer a world where Shinji and Asuka are newborn Adam and Eve, or else the only residents of an eclipsed reality, or else the first of a long series of reborn individuals? Pick your favorite, but they are there. Do you think that "how disgusting / gross" means that Shinji is still immature, or rather weak or pathetic, or that Asuka maintains her conflictual attitude of superiority? Choose your favorite, but he had already made the most courageous choice of living as individual and she had already gently showed her affection, which already stopped his burst of frustrated violence. Do you prefer to believe that Shinji and Asuka will meet again, through high school or university, after their first chance handshake? Maybe they will share a life, raise children and taste the good and the bad of long days together. Or maybe they will never meet again, there are so many things to do and so many people to meet. Or maybe one of them will have a car accident shortly afterwards. Or maybe they will first stay together, but then depart because there is no communion. Do you think that she acted slightly less arrogantly and he acted slightly more confidently? Perhaps. At the end, whatever it may be, they chose to be there, because it was worth it. What did lead Shinji and Asuka to made such choice? Perhaps a caring mother once forgotten and re-met, or never forgotten, or a single love gesture from a father otherwise alien, or the unconscious awareness or the hope that deep under all flaws, misunderstandings and weaknesses they could share mutual feelings. It does not matter, what matters is that this sparkle of positivity is sufficient to wash away the shadows of doubt, indifference and abandon. Shinji proves much stronger than his father, Asuka proves to be much more than a mere synchronization rate, in any event. Ernest Hemingway, in For Whom the Bells Tolls, expresses the feeling that the world is worth fighting for. In Evangelion, this concept and the concept that life is worth living for is questioned and challenged all over, but as alluring as the alternative would sound, at the end life wins. Nothing is for free and life is not painless, but we must accept the challenge. Both medals and scars contribute to our existence, imperfect but worth it. Best, A.

[]:             Quote Post by ObsessiveMathsFreak » Wed Jul 17, 2013 5:58 pm Thanks for the personal perspective on the show. Interesting to see someone opening with an assessment instead of questions as well. It's interesting to see someone finally draw positive connections between the endings of the Manga and the show. I never thought of the Manga as delivering a message of hope until I read this.
